System Architecture

Linux - Introduction  
• Linux is a Unix clone written from scratch by Linus Torvalds  
• Unix is a multitasking; multi-user computer operating system  
• originally developed in 1969 by the group of AT&T employees at Bell Labs.  
• Linux and Unix strives to be POSIX compliant  
• Open-source kernel  
The Linux Philosophy  
Each program should be focused on a single, well-defined task.  
Build small, modular programs  
Do one thing, and do it well  
Use text as the universal interface  
Everything is a file  
Linux tools typically take text as input and output text.  
Devices, processes, configuration—Linux represents all of them  
as files in the filesystem  
Make programs work together  
Configuration over magic  
Output of one program becomes input to another  
Linux tends to avoid hiding things.  
Linux’s open-source nature encourages contributions, forks,  
Open systems encourage innovation  
experiments, and learning.  
Core Framing: Linux is a Kernel  
Linux  
“Linux is the kernel—the  
privileged core that manages  
CPU scheduling, memory,  
filesystems, devices, and  
Operating  
System  
User  
system calls.”  
Space  
• A distribution bundles kernel + user space + packaging + policies.  
• Linux is the kernel; GNU/Linux is the complete operating system; Ubuntu is a distribution.  
Important to know!  
Kernel ≠ OS ≠ Distribution  
• The kernel exposes syscalls;  
• user space provides libraries/tools;  
• distributions package them.

OS

• Device drivers in kernel space

Understanding API, POSIX and C Library  
Call to  
printf()  
printf() definition  
in C Library  
write()  
in C Library  
sys_write()  
system call  
C Application  
C Library  
Kernel  
• Applications are programmed against an Application Programming Interface(API), not directly to system  
calls.  
• Application Programming Interfaces in the Unix based OS is based on the POSIX standard.  
• The C library implements the main API on Unix based systems, including the standard C library and the  
system call interface.  
Managing I/O devices  
• Linux manages I/O devices through a layered  
architecture that abstracts hardware  
complexity and provides a unified interface for  
applications.  
User Applications  
Shared Libraries (libc)  
System Call Interface  
• User Space - Applications make system calls  
like read(), write(), ioctl().  
• VFS (Virtual File System) - Provides a unified  
interface for files and devices.  
Virtual File System  
Device Drivers  
• Device Driver - Translates generic I/O requests  
into hardware-specific operations.  
• Hardware - Actual device performing the I/O.  
Input/Output Devices  
An example- I/O devices

read() system call

system call

User applications and its interface with Kernel  
Layer  
User app  
C library  
Syscall  
VFS  
Example  
printf()  
write() wrapper  
sys_write()  
file ops dispatch  
HW-specific write  
Driver  
• Linux recognizes hardware device as file and hence each hardware device is  
registered with Kernel file system by means of VFS.  
• Device drivers implement device specific functions for each system call function  
and helps device to register with VFS.  
Device Drivers  
A device driver is a kernel module that allows the Linux operating system to communicate  
with hardware devices.  
User-Space Application  
Use-Space Applications  
• Calls standard system calls like open(), read(), write().  
System Calls → VFS Layer  
• VFS acts as an abstraction layer and routes these calls to the  
correct driver.  
file_operations Structure  
System call Interface  
• Contains pointers to device-specific implementations for open,  
read, write, etc.  
VFS Layer  
File  
operation  
structure  
Device Driver  
Linux recognizes hardware  
device as file, hence each  
hardware device is registered  
with Linux Kernel using VFS  
• Implements hardware-specific logic and registers itself with  
VFS using APIs like cdev_add() and device_create().  
Hardware Device  
Device  
Drivers  
• Actual physical device accessed through driver logic.  
Hardware Device  
Understanding Embedded Linux Architecture  
• Embedded Linux is built around the same Linux Kernel which is apart of most of  
Linux OS and is available under https://kernel.org/.  
• Requires additional packages beyond the original Linux kernel which depends  
upon the embedded use case.  
Embedded Application  
Creating Target Linux System for  
Embedded Devices:  
Linux File System  
Linux Kernel  
• Identify Various Components as per  
Embedded Use case.  
Board  
Support  
Package  
• Configure and Build the Linux Kernel  
Device  
Drivers  
for specific architecture  
Bootloader  
• Build Root file system  
Embedded Hardware  
(x86, Arm, RISC-V)  
• Setup boot loaders, BSPs etc.  
Distributions: Ubuntu vs Minimal Embedded Rootfs  
A distribution = kernel + userland + packaging + policies.  
A curated collection of:  
• Kernel  
• User space (libc + tools)  
• Package manager  
• Init system & policies  
• Configuration defaults  
•
Ubuntu/Debian: general-purpose, rich tooling, package  
manager (apt), larger footprint  
• Yocto/OpenEmbedded: build system to craft custom  
embedded distributions  
Summary  
• Linux is a Kernel (Kernel ≠ OS ≠ Distribution)  
• Linux = Kernel + Syscalls + Drivers  
• Applications run in user space using libc → syscalls → kernel to access hardware.  
• User space = libc + tools + apps  
• VFS makes everything a file, Devices, processes, configuration — accessed via VFS abstractions.  
• Embedded Linux = Bootloader + Kernel + Rootfs + BSP  
• Built using: bootloader + kernel + rootfs + drivers/BSP.  
• Distributions like Ubuntu = general-purpose; Yocto = custom minimal rootfs
